headline: Clemson escapes Boston College in defensive slugfest, 21-20
body: Clemson's defense bent but did not break against a Boston College offense that generated 405 total yards, holding firm in moments that mattered most to secure a one-point victory in Week 2. The Eagles moved the ball with efficiency throughout, yet repeatedly stalled in the red zone, a pattern that would define the afternoon and ultimately cost them a win they had every chance to take.

James Cortez orchestrated a surgical performance from the Clemson pocket, completing 19 of 30 passes for 252 yards and a touchdown while managing the game without costly mistakes. His efficiency—not volume—was the story. Boston College's Quinn Leavitt threw for 292 yards and two touchdowns, but the sheer yardage masked an underlying issue: the Eagles could not convert opportunities into points when it mattered. Clemson's ground game stayed quiet with just 114 rushing yards, yet Adams Jr. punched in a touchdown that proved decisive.

The turning point arrived late, when Clemson's defense stepped up with the game hanging in the balance. Boston College had momentum and field position, but the Eagles stalled short of the end zone one too many time. Clemson's defensive unit, led by Mickens' eight tackles, suffocated drives that looked explosive on the surface. In a game where both teams left yards on the field, the team that left fewer points on the board won the day.
turningPoint: Boston College's inability to convert a late scoring opportunity inside Clemson territory allowed the Tigers to preserve their one-point lead in the final minutes.

unsungHero.name: Mickens
unsungHero.why: His eight tackles set the tone for a defense that held Boston College to 20 points despite being out-gained by 60 yards; the real work was preventing explosive plays, not recording sacks.
